name: Release Conformance Organ
known_blockers: Ledger established at this baseline (Slice 25); the strict gate stays non-green until every organ below turns green and Slice 40 final release proof lands.

name: Human Sovereign Identity
known_blockers: Slice 26 landed session_generation (stale sessions now fail closed at IdentityService.get_authenticated_principal) and constitution_digest stamping on Principal, plus threading through MissionContract/CapabilityBinding/ActionEnvelope. Still missing: HTTP-layer end-to-end enforcement of a constitution-digest mismatch, and read-only status during degraded identity is not yet a distinct code path.

name: Constitutional Kernel
known_blockers: ConstitutionSnapshotV1 now exists (typed, versioned, digested, foundation laws immutable-by-validator) via aios.domain.governance.constitution. Still missing: durable cross-restart persistence/ratification (Slice 37 territory), PolicyKernel still reads the old aios.policy.constitution.Constitution facade rather than ConstitutionSnapshotV1, and no decision path rejects execution on a constitution-digest mismatch yet.

name: Emergency Stop Organ (full boundary hard-wiring)
known_blockers: Slice 27 closed 5 confirmed-missing boundaries: intelligence gateway (local + cloud calls), skill activation/reuse, maintenance scan/repair (service layer, not just the pre-existing route-layer checks), backup restore, and capability consume (defense-in-depth alongside issue()). Still open: constitutional amendment activation (organ does not exist until Slice 37); the route-layer duplication across routes/council.py and routes/maintenance.py with inconsistent EmergencyStopError handling (503 vs uncaught) is not yet centralized; and mission state transitions beyond create/start (approve/reject/verify/complete/rollback) are not individually re-checked.

name: Project Understanding Organ
known_blockers: Tier-1 closure pass: the organ's other stated gap -- 'no durable store persists a ProjectPassportV1 across process restarts' -- is now resolved. New ProjectPassportStore (aios/infrastructure/memory/human_representation_store.py, migration 0008, scope="human_representation") gives ProjectPassportV1 real, append-only, digest-verified history across restarts, mirroring GovernanceAmendmentStore's established convention. Verified directly: a fresh store instance over the same file sees the exact history a prior instance wrote (the real cross-restart proof this gap named), and a tampered row is caught at read time via digest mismatch. Still genuinely missing (unchanged, honest scope note -- this pass did not build a new scanner): invariants and explicit_human_decisions still have no source in the existing harvest_project_passport() scanner and default empty rather than being fabricated, exactly as the domain contract's own docstring already documented before this pass.

name: Communication and Human-State Interpreter
known_blockers: HumanStateHypothesis now exists (typed, grants_authority and user_correctable pinned literals) with classify_human_state(), a small deterministic regex classifier in the same style as aios.core.alignment.infer_communication_mode -- this organ had no prior art at all before Slice 28. Still missing: it is not wired into any live turn/conversation path yet, has no persistence, and the classifier itself is a first-pass heuristic that has not been measured against real operator messages.

name: Human Representative Context Compiler
known_blockers: RepresentativeContextV1 now exists (typed, digested, cloud/local privacy-minimized) with compile_representative_context() composing ConstitutionSnapshotV1/OperatorPreferenceV1/ProjectPassportV1/CorrectionRecordV1 into one immutable packet. Cloud target scrubs free-text fields via the existing SecretPolicy and structurally withholds relevant_memory_refs; contradicted/superseded preferences are excluded; stale passports are flagged in uncertainty. Still missing: nothing calls this compiler yet -- IntelligenceRequest and ModelCallRequest still carry only a bare prompt/principal_id with no context_digest field (confirmed by a red-first test), so no model call is actually forced through this compiler. That wiring is Slice 30 (Universal Intelligence Gateway) territory.

name: Universal Intelligence Gateway
known_blockers: Reconciliation pass item 2 (prompted by an explicit operator request to reconcile organs 40/41, which surfaced this as the natural next item): Council Planner/King LLM reasoning is now genuinely routed through route_intelligence_request() via aios/council/gateway_reasoning.py -- the first production wiring of route_intelligence_request() outside its own tests, and the first production wiring of Council's LLM slots at all (both PlannerQueen.__init__(llm=...) and CouncilOrchestrator's king_complete= were previously always None in every real construction site; grep-confirmed zero production hits before this). Every Council completion is now emergency-stop gated and produces a real, auditable context_digest. Investigated and fixed a real regression this surfaced: test_council_origination.py/test_royal_decree.py exercised this newly-live path with COUNCIL_REASONING=True and a real enrolled test operator, making a real ~11s Ollama call -- neutralized with config.COUNCIL_REASONING=False in those tests' setup, matching test_api.py's established FakeLLM-override convention for exactly this class of problem. Still not universal (this organ's own name): chat (/api/v1/chat) and the agentic forge (/api/generate) remain unwired -- both are STREAMING call sites (stream_chat_chunks/ToolAgent.run), while route_intelligence_request()'s model_call callback is synchronous request/response only. Wiring those would mean giving up token-by-token streaming unless the gateway grows a streaming variant first -- deliberately not attempted in this pass rather than accepting that UX regression silently. The two other competing gateway-shaped implementations (aios.runtime.intelligence_gateway.IntelligenceGateway, aios.application.models.hiring_service.IntelligenceHiringService) also remain unreconciled.

name: Model Registry and Capability Passport
known_blockers: ModelPassportV1 now exists (typed, evidence-backed shape: qualified_roles/disallowed_roles/tool_protocol_status/admission_status) wrapping the existing aios.core.router.Provider pure-data model with the role-scoped admission concept it does not carry. is_admitted_for_role()/can_drive_tools()/is_stale_for_version() are real, deterministic checks. Still missing: nothing actually runs a qualification suite yet -- every ModelPassportV1 in this codebase must be hand-constructed; real qualification against the live granite3.2:2b Ollama model is Slice 32, and no durable store persists a passport across restarts.

Reconciliation pass item 4: the same live-registry drift documented in organ 37 lives here too -- ModelPassportV1's admission concept is real and typed, but the actual running LocalWorkforceRegistry (data/aios_memory.db, this machine's genuine operational state) is a separate, older, untyped admission store (local_worker_models table, allowed_job_profiles_json) that was found claiming an evidence-contradicted admission for granite3.2:2b -- corrected using the new aios/application/local_workforce/qualification_evidence.py (see organ 37). These two admission concepts (ModelPassportV1 vs. LocalWorkforceRegistry) still are not unified into one authority -- a real, separate architectural gap this item did not attempt to close.

name: Cloud Budget and Provider-Health Organ
known_blockers: ProviderHealthSnapshot + ProviderHealthTracker now exist: a real, deterministic closed/open/half-open circuit breaker driven entirely by caller-reported call outcomes (in-memory per-process, matching aios.runtime.budget_guard.BudgetGuard's own established convention, not a new durability promise). Unknown budget/cost is represented as None, never fabricated as zero. Still missing: nothing wires real provider calls (deps.py client factories, router_wiring) through this tracker yet, so it tracks nothing in production; no durable persistence across restarts; and BudgetGuard itself is unchanged -- the two are not yet unified into one budget+health view.

name: Clerical Job Contract and Dispatcher
known_blockers: dispatch_clerical_job() now exists: deterministic code always wins first (Granite is never consulted when a parser can already answer), an unqualified or failed-qualification model always escalates to frontier regardless of self-reported confidence, and only a qualified model's own low-confidence result routes to a human. Still missing: nothing in aios.application.local_workforce.service.LocalWorkforceService (the real production run_advisory_job() path) calls this dispatcher yet -- it is a standalone, tested decision function, not wired into the live request path.

name: Local Model Qualification and Health
known_blockers: A REAL qualification suite already existed (aios.domain.local_workforce.qualifier.QualificationSuite, 12 model cases + reliability/resource/concurrency/timeout gates) and Slice 32 actually ran it 3x live against the real granite3.2:2b model via Ollama in this environment -- exact tag, digest 9d79a41f..., hardware profile, and elapsed time recorded in release/slice32/granite-qualification-live.json. Honest finding, not hidden: all 3 runs failed the same way -- the model reliably passes 15/16 checks but consistently fails "summarisation" because it substitutes its own field name (observed: service_status) instead of the instructed exact field name (summary), confirmed reproducible across 5 additional raw sampling attempts (1/5 used the correct name). This is a genuine, reproducible model-reliability gap, not a test bug or a synthetic fixture -- the organ stays yellow because the live evidence itself shows the model does not yet qualify, which is exactly what a truthful qualification gate is for. Follow-up: either the qualifier needs a schema-normalising retry (Slice 32's own "invalid JSON retries within a bounded count" requirement, not yet built), or this specific case needs loosening to accept any single-field object regardless of field name -- a real design decision, not made here.

Reconciliation pass item 4 ('narrow Granite's admitted profile honestly'): grounded by checking THIS machine's real, running local registry state (data/aios_memory.db -- gitignored, genuine operational state, not a test fixture), not just the checked-in evidence file. Found a real, live dishonesty: the registry's actual granite3.2:2b row claimed admission_reason='Passed all qualification checks' and allowed_job_profiles including summarise/triage/format_report -- directly contradicted by this organ's own checked-in release/slice32/granite-qualification-live.json, which shows 0/3 runs passing all 16 checks and summarisation failing in every run. triage/format_report were never tested by this suite at all, so admitting them had zero evidence either way. Root cause: LocalWorkforceRegistry.update_profiles()/update_admission() store whatever a caller passes on trust -- nothing anywhere in this codebase ever derived an admitted profile from real qualification evidence. Built the narrow, honest piece of that gap: aios/application/local_workforce/qualification_evidence.py (evidence_backed_profiles()/unsupported_claimed_profiles()) maps only the 4 LocalJobProfile values with an unambiguous, name-matched test_id in the current suite (extract/classify/summarise/cluster) and requires a mapped test to pass in EVERY recorded run, not just one, before calling a profile evidence-backed -- verified directly against the real evidence file in tests/test_local_workforce_qualification_evidence.py, confirming it correctly narrows to {extract, classify, cluster} and flags the exact drift found live. Used it to correct the actual running registry row (data/aios_memory.db, local-only, not committed) to the honest set. NOT built: a full automatic qualification->admission derivation pipeline -- most LocalJobProfile values (triage, format_report, prepare_briefing, select_skill, parameterise_skill, validate_structure, summarise_disagreement, explain_route, check_context_completeness) have no corresponding test in the current 16-check suite at all, so there is nothing to auto-derive for them without first extending the suite itself -- a genuine, separate design task, not attempted here.

Tier-1 closure pass (organ 37 specifically): built the schema-normalising retry the prior blocker named as the correct fix (not the loosening alternative). QualificationSuite._run_case now retries once (bounded, configurable via max_schema_retries) ONLY on a validator-stage rejection -- valid, safe JSON in the wrong shape -- feeding the exact rejection reason back to the model; a timeout, LLM error, oversized output, forbidden value, or invalid/non-object JSON is never retried. Verified with 6 new fake-client tests (recovery, bounded exhaustion, non-retryable failures untouched, disable-via-zero) in tests/domain/test_local_workforce_qualifier.py. Re-ran live against the real granite3.2:2b model 3x with the retry active -- see release/slice32/granite-qualification-live-organ37-retry.json. Honest result: a real improvement (1 of 3 runs now passes all 16 checks, versus 0 of 3 in the pre-retry baseline), but not a fix -- 2 of 3 runs still fail summarisation even after the corrective retry (attempts=2, still the wrong field name). The organ stays yellow: the retry is genuinely built and live-verified, but granite3.2:2b still does not reliably qualify.

name: Durable Local-Clerk Provenance and Continuity Organ
known_blockers: Reconciliation pass item 3: the organ's own stated gap -- 'LocalWorkforceService.run_advisory_job() doesn't call this store yet, so no real job is persisted in production' -- is now resolved. run_advisory_job() is a thin wrapper around the original logic (renamed _execute_advisory_job, body byte-for-byte unchanged, zero validation-logic risk) that records every real job's request, model call, and result via LocalWorkforceProvenanceStore once execution completes -- honestly, including refusals (no admitted model) and schema-validation failures, not only successes. get_local_workforce_service() (aios/api/deps.py) now constructs and injects a real store at config.LOCAL_WORKFORCE_PROVENANCE_DB_PATH (new config constant), so this is genuinely live in the production DI path, not just available. Fail-safe by construction: provenance_store defaults to None and recording is skipped entirely when absent, matching every other optional-dependency pattern in this codebase. New tests/test_local_workforce_service_provenance.py (5 tests: no-store no-op, successful job recorded, rejection recorded, schema-failure recorded, two jobs don't collide). While grounding this, found and reported (not fixed here, flagged as a separate task) a real pre-existing bug unrelated to this change: three tests in test_r15_new_blockers.py's TestBlocker9LocalJobSchema silently never exercised the schema-validation code they claim to test, because a bare MagicMock() registry always satisfies hasattr(registry, 'dependency'), triggering a real-registry fallback that made every job 'rejected' rather than 'failed' -- their weak assertion (status != 'completed') passed either way. Still missing: dispatch_clerical_job() (Slice 32) itself remains unwired -- a full integration needs a QualificationResult tracked per model, which isn't stored anywhere in the registry today; inventing that under time pressure was deliberately not attempted here rather than forcing a shallow/fake integration.

name: Multi-Model Deliberation and Dissent Organ
known_blockers: Reconciliation pass item 2: Council Queens' LLM slots are wired but were never supplied a client in production -- this is now genuinely resolved. aios/council/gateway_reasoning.py's build_council_llm_client() supplies a real, gateway-routed LLMClient to both PlannerQueen(llm=...) and CouncilOrchestrator(king_complete=...) at both real construction sites (aios/api/routes/council.py's _run_council_deliberation and _run_council_execution). Fail-closed by design: returns None (fully deterministic Council, unchanged prior behaviour) when config.COUNCIL_REASONING is off or no sovereign operator is enrolled yet. Live-verified via the browser bridge that the real origination endpoint's pre-existing auth boundary (require_privileged_operator) is unaffected. Still open: this makes a single model's reasoning live, not the DeliberationRecord multi-model dissent machinery (Slice 34) itself -- Council Queens' LLM slots being live doesn't yet mean a live DeliberationRecord with independent positions gets created from a real Council mission; that wiring is a separate, larger piece of work.

name: Isolated Workspace and Executor (live proof)

live_evidence.description: CI run https://github.com/swap821/ai-editor/actions/runs/29890416231 (commit db0441144db9): a real docker-compose Executor Service container was started, and aios.application.governance.runtime_proof._probe_executor made a genuine HTTP call through StructuredExecutorClient to submit and execute an isolated job inside it -- verified real output confirming uid=65534 (non-root), network=blocked, and outside=blocked (no workspace escape). Separately, tests/test_executor_integration.py ran python -m pytest *inside* the real container via `docker compose run`, and test_missing_private_executor_is_refused proved there is no host-process fallback when the service is absent. This is genuine runtime evidence, not a fixture: reviewed the proof source directly (not just the passing CI job name) to confirm it makes real network calls against a real container rather than mocking the executor client.

known_blockers: The specific blocker recorded at Slice 35/40 -- 'Docker unavailability independently blocks the executor runtime probe' -- is now resolved: real CI evidence exists (see live_evidence above), reviewed at the source-code level to confirm it is genuinely live, not mocked. Still yellow, for two real remaining gaps found by checking the plan's own 10-point bar directly rather than assuming CI-green implies full green: (1) no frontend surface shows executor status truthfully -- grepped the entire frontend workbench/superbrain source tree for 'executor' and found zero matches; (2) failure and timeout are tested (test_missing_private_executor_is_refused, test_private_executor_timeout_is_refused) but there is no test specifically covering executor *restart* resilience. Both are real, scoped, single-commit-sized follow-on work, not something to half-build here. This evidence was produced by CI, not by anything run interactively in this session -- this local sandbox still has no Docker daemon.

name: Promotion, Checkpoint and Rollback (live proof)
known_blockers: Checked directly against the new CI evidence before assuming it helps: the same CI run that proves organ 40 does NOT resolve this organ's specific gap. Read aios/application/governance/runtime_proof.py's _probe_staging_and_promotion source directly -- its promotion_authority/staged_workspaces/verification_and_recovery proofs all call PromotionAuthority.promote() with hand-injected lambda stubs for create_checkpoint (returns a fake string id, creates nothing) and restore_checkpoint (either `lambda *_: True`, doing nothing, or a hardcoded rewrite of a known string) -- this tests PromotionAuthority's orchestration contract with fakes, never touching Docker or a live daemon at all, and never exercising the real CheckpointAuthority/WorkspacePromotionRuntime persistence path. This is exactly the 'not a mocked one' gap this organ's blocker already named before this update. tests/test_executor_integration.py (the genuinely live proof organ 40 now cites) only covers isolation (non-root/network/timeout/missing-service refusal) -- zero overlap with promotion, checkpoint, or rollback. Construction still exists (organs 15/16, real CheckpointAuthority/WorkspacePromotionRuntime); an authoritative post-promotion receipt and a genuine (non-mocked) rollback-restores-exact-bytes proof against a live daemon remain unbuilt.

name: Recovery and Resumption
known_blockers: MissionTransitionJournal now exists and is genuinely durable and restart-safe -- verified directly (not just asserted) for all 9 crash points in the brief's failure matrix: a fresh journal instance over the same db file after a simulated restart correctly reports the exact same current_state and can resume forward from it. Re-appending the current transition is a true no-op (idempotent retry cannot fork the lineage); any other out-of-order transition is refused. A real table-name collision with the pre-existing coarse MissionState transition-audit table (both named mission_transitions) was caught by the full regression suite before commit and fixed by renaming to mission_execution_transitions -- the two are genuinely different concepts (coarse lifecycle audit vs. fine-grained execution/promotion pipeline journal) that happened to collide on name, not a duplicate.

Reconciliation pass item 5 (mission-journal half): grounded properly before attempting to wire anything, and found this is materially larger and riskier than 'add a journal call to MissionService'. The journal's own vocabulary (MISSION_CREATED, WORKSPACE_CREATED, EXECUTION_SUBMITTED, EXECUTION_COMPLETED, VERIFIED, CHECKPOINT_CREATED, PROMOTION_STARTED, PROMOTED, POST_PROMOTION_VERIFIED, COMPLETED) is a DIFFERENT, finer-grained state machine than MissionService's coarse MissionState (DRAFT, DELIBERATING, AWAITING_APPROVAL, RUNNING, ...) -- confirmed by the journal's own docstring, which says it exists specifically because a mission can sit in the single coarse RUNNING state for the entire workspace -> executor -> verification -> checkpoint -> promotion sequence with no durable record of which sub-step actually completed. Read aios/application/promotion/authority.py's promote() directly: it only covers the back half of the journal's vocabulary (roughly CHECKPOINT_CREATED -> PROMOTED/FAILED/ROLLED_BACK); the earlier steps (WORKSPACE_CREATED, EXECUTION_SUBMITTED, EXECUTION_COMPLETED, VERIFIED) live in separate classes entirely (StagedWorkspaceManager, WorkerFoundry/Executor, VerificationAuthority). A faithful wiring therefore spans 5-6 files, each needing a correctly-sequenced append() call -- and append() enforces strict ordering (_validate_next), so a wrong insertion point anywhere in that chain raises rather than silently degrading, with real risk of breaking production promotion flow rather than just failing a test. This session's Docker daemon is unavailable (see organs 40/41), so the real happy-path sequence through an actual worker/executor/verification cycle cannot be exercised end to end to validate the wiring is even correctly ordered. Deliberately not attempted under time pressure -- same category of honest deferral as dispatch_clerical_job() (organ 38) and chat/forge streaming (organ 32): a real, scoped, higher-risk task for its own dedicated pass with either live Docker access or much more careful per-file staging than a single reconciliation-pass commit allows.

name: Local Skill Reuse, Confidence and Demotion
Golden Mission and Endurance Evaluation
known_blockers: The brief's golden release cohort (12 governed frontier missions across 2 real cloud providers, 4 candidate skills, 3 verified local reuses each, forced-refusal/version-drift-suspension/failed-reuse-demotion/human-revocation/cloud-outage/uncertain-escalation cases) requires hours of real governed mission execution against live frontier providers. Checked realistically rather than assumed: this is not achievable or appropriate to run autonomously in this environment/pass -- it needs real cloud credentials, real mission budgets, and genuine multi-hour wall-clock execution, not something to simulate or fake. Recorded honestly as not attempted, not silently skipped. The individual mechanisms this cohort would exercise (demotion, revocation, suspension) are real and unit-tested in organ 43; what's missing is the live, expensive, end-to-end proof run itself.

name: Constitutional Amendment Authority
known_blockers: Confirmed genuinely missing (grep for ConstitutionalAmendment/amendment_authority found nothing but this slice's own additions) before building. ConstitutionalAmendmentProposalV1 + propose_amendment()/critique_amendment()/simulate_amendment()/ratify_amendment()/activate_amendment()/rollback_amendment()/reject_amendment() now exist. ratify_amendment() can only be satisfied by a real, already-consumed capability bound to the exact operator and to CONSTITUTIONAL_AMENDMENT_RATIFY_ACTION -- models/workers have no path to produce one. Foundation-law-touching proposals are refused even with a valid capability. activate_amendment() reuses Slice 26's build_constitution_snapshot() chaining machinery directly and is now gated by emergency_stop -- closing a boundary Slice 27 flagged before this organ existed. Verified directly: existing MissionContract instances keep their original constitution_digest after activation (frozen contract, never mutated), and rollback refuses a snapshot that isn't the exact predecessor.

Reconciliation pass item 6: durable persistence now exists. GovernanceAmendmentStore (aios/infrastructure/governance/sqlite_store.py, migration 0007, scope="governance") records every propose/critique/simulate/ratify/activate/reject/rollback transition as its own append-only, digest-verified revision row rather than upserting current state in place -- matching the correction-lineage convention already established for operator preferences (Slice 28). Verified end to end in tests/test_governance_amendment_store.py by driving the real propose_amendment/critique_amendment/ratify_amendment functions and confirming the full transition history survives a save/read round trip, not just the final status; also verified a tampered row (edited outside the store) is detected at read time via digest mismatch, the same pattern as LocalWorkforceProvenanceStore (Slice 33). Still missing (unchanged scope -- amendments.py's own docstring already names this as deliberate follow-up, not assumed by Slice 37): no aios/api/routes/ HTTP surface, and CONSTITUTIONAL_AMENDMENT_RATIFY_ACTION is still not registered in the real ActionType/PolicyKernel capability-issuance routing table, so no real capability can actually be issued for it in production yet. Deliberately not attempted here: wiring that route touches the same PolicyKernel routing table every other action type depends on, and a wrong entry risks breaking existing action routing -- a correctly scoped, separate task, not a reconciliation-pass addition.

name: Constitutional Learning Organ
known_blockers: Confirmed genuinely missing before building. GovernanceLessonV1 + propose_lesson()/lesson_to_amendment_proposal()/require_all_simulations_pass() now exist, feeding Slice 37's amendment pipeline directly rather than duplicating it. The one rule this organ exists to serve -- GAGOS may learn its sovereignty mechanisms are weak but may never itself propose reducing human authority -- is enforced by a deterministic keyword screen (assert_never_reduces_human_authority) checked against both the lesson's own text and the diff text independently, verified directly against 8 distinct authority-reducing phrasings. A full proposal -> simulation -> human-ratification -> rollback-capable-activation path was run end to end in a test, reusing Slice 37's real ratify_amendment/activate_amendment/rollback_amendment unchanged.

Reconciliation pass item 6: GovernanceAmendmentStore also persists GovernanceLessonV1 (same table family, same append-only digest-verified revision-per-row pattern as the amendment side). Verified end to end in tests/test_governance_amendment_store.py by driving the real propose_lesson -> lesson_to_amendment_proposal pipeline and confirming both the lesson's own status transition (proposed -> amendment_drafted) and its amendment_proposal_id linkage survive a real save/read round trip through the store, plus tamper detection on a lesson row edited outside the store. Still missing (unchanged scope): the 9 named adversarial simulations remain a required catalog this module checks results FOR, not simulations it runs; no HTTP route exists, and drafting a lesson into a proposal still inherits organ 45's same capability-issuance-table gap before it could ever be ratified in production.

name: Read-Model and Projection Organ
known_blockers: ConstitutionProjection/EmergencyStopProjection/ProviderHealthProjection/ApprovalProjection now exist, built on the pre-existing MetricEnvelope/MetricStatus provenance primitive (not a new one) with real projector functions (project_constitution/project_emergency_stop/project_provider_health/project_approval) verified directly against real Slice 26 ConstitutionSnapshotV1, the real EmergencyStopState, a real ProviderHealthSnapshot, and the real production ApprovedAction from aios/core/approvals.py's ApprovalStore. Missing/None source state renders every field UNAVAILABLE, never a fabricated default (verified: no constitution loaded, unknown provider budget, unsupplied approval fields). Still yellow (honest scope note, not an oversight): these projectors are not yet called by any live API route or wired into the frontend -- that requires a real browser operator walkthrough this autonomous session cannot perform (no human present to review a live UI session), so it was not attempted rather than claimed.

name: Observability and Health Organ
known_blockers: No unified correlation-ID tracked telemetry across model calls, clerk, executor, mission queue, verification, promotion, rollback, and emergency-stop exists yet.

Reconciliation pass item 9: grounded, not built. aios/operations/tracing.py's TraceContext/new_trace_context/bind_trace_context/get_trace_context are real, tested code (see tests/test_operations.py::test_trace_context_rejects_unbounded_or_invalid_header_values) but confirmed via grep across the whole tree to still be unbound and unused outside their own test -- no HTTP middleware constructs one from real request headers, and no model call, clerk job, executor submission, mission-queue dequeue, verification, promotion, rollback, or emergency-stop path binds one via bind_trace_context(). Wiring that in is a real, cross-cutting, architecturally-invasive change (FastAPI middleware, propagation across every async call chain named above) -- correctly scoped as its own dedicated slice, not attempted as part of a reconciliation-pass commit.

name: Installation, Configuration and Key Authority
known_blockers: Key rotation, a bounded grace period, and truthful Ollama-absence handling are not fully implemented.

Reconciliation pass item 9: grounded, not built. ActionType.SECURITY_TOKENS_ROTATE and an audit_key_rotate route-authority entry exist in the policy kernel's routing table, but confirmed no function anywhere actually rotates a key -- there is no real secret-material rotation implementation to wire up, only a named, unimplemented action type. Implementing real key rotation (generating new credentials, safely invalidating old ones without breaking already-authenticated sessions, distributing new keys to already-running processes) is a genuine, security-sensitive design task, not a wiring gap -- correctly scoped as its own dedicated slice, not attempted here.
